coredata.dc:title: A novel luminescence sensor based on porous molecularly imprinted polymer-ZnS quantum dots for selective recognition of paclitaxel
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Jianwei
Chen, Longqi
Zhu, Yawei
Wang, Xiqing
Wu, Xiaodan
Fu, Yujie
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor was constructed successfully for selective recognition of PTX by introducing methacryl polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (M-POSS) to add porous structure. The obtained ﬂuorescent sensors were prepared by grafting MIP shell on the surface of silane functionalized Mn: ZnS Quantum Dots (QDs) by a free radical polymerization process. The binding properties and selectivity of the MIP-QDs were demonstrated by adsorption capacity obtained based on ﬂuorescence spectroscopy. These MIP-based ﬂuorescent sensors showed good dispersibility and high stability in solution. Under the optimized conditions, the binding experiment demonstrated the ﬂuorescent intensity of the porous ﬂuorescent sensors was obviously decreased with the increase of PTX in the concentration range 0.1–2.0 μM and the detection limit was 0.043 μM. These results demonstrated a new type of molecularly imprinted fluorescent porous nanoparticles with potential applications for selective recognition of PTX biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymers
Quantum dot
Porous structure
Fluorescent sensor
Paclitaxel

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Mn: ZnS quantum dots
Condition optimization on the fluorescence properties of the POSS-MIP/QDs
Sensitivity and selectivity of the POSS-MIP/QDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation process and possible detection principle of the POSS-MIP/QDs.
FT-IR spectra of QDs(a), QDs@SiO2(b), MIP/QDs(c), POSS-MIP/QDs(d).
TEM images of (a)QDs, (b) QDs@SiO2, (c)POSS-MIP/QDs, (d) HAADF-STEM images of POSS-MIP/QDs, (e-i) Element mapping image, followed by C, Si, S, Zn, and overlay images.
XRD spectra of QDs(a), QDs@SiO2(b), MIP/QDs(c), POSS-MIP/QDs(d).
XPS spectra of QDs(a), QDs@SiO2(b), MIP/QDs(c), POSS-MIP/QDs(d).
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm and the pore size distribution (inset) of POSS-MIP/QDs, POSS-NIP/QDs, MIP/QDs, NIP/QDs.
Effect of pH on fluorescence intensities of POSS-MIP/QDs in the presence of 0.5 μM PTX.
Time response of MIP/QDs and POSS-MIP/QDs in the presence of 1 μM PTX.

Fluorescence emission spectra POSS-MIP/QDs (a), POSS-NIP/QDs (b), MIP/QDs (c) and NIP/QDs (d) with the addition of the different concentrations of PTX (0.1-2 μM), respectively. The inset graphs show ﬂuorescence intensity corresponding Stern-Volmer plots.
Fluorescence quenching amounts of POSS-MIP/QDs and POSS-NIP/QDs for PTX and its analogues.
Reversible recovery cycles upon the repeated addition and removal of PTX in the presence of POSS-MIP/QDs.
Surface Properties of POSS-MIP/QDs, POSS-NIP/QDs, MIP/QDs, NIP/QDs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Surface area calculated from the nitrogen adsorption isotherms at 77.3 K using the BET equation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Surface area calculated from nitrogen adsorption isotherms at 77.3 K using Langmuir equation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Micropore volume calculated from the t-Plot equation at 77.3 K.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Pore volume calculated from the nitrogen isotherm at P/P0 = 0.998, 77.3 K.

head.ce:title: A novel luminescence sensor based on porous molecularly imprinted polymer-ZnS quantum dots for selective recognition of paclitaxel

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 1 Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Materials Research Center, College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 2 Polymer Materials Research Center, College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Forest Bio-preparation, Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Forest Bio-preparation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 3 Engineering Research Center of Forest Bio-preparation, Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor was constructed successfully for selective recognition of PTX by introducing methacryl polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (M-POSS) to add porous structure. The obtained ﬂuorescent sensors were prepared by grafting MIP shell on the surface of silane functionalized Mn: ZnS Quantum Dots (QDs) by a free radical polymerization process. The binding properties and selectivity of the MIP-QDs were demonstrated by adsorption capacity obtained based on ﬂuorescence spectroscopy. These MIP-based ﬂuorescent sensors showed good dispersibility and high stability in solution. Under the optimized conditions, the binding experiment demonstrated the ﬂuorescent intensity of the porous ﬂuorescent sensors was obviously decreased with the increase of PTX in the concentration range 0.1–2.0 μM and the detection limit was 0.043 μM. These results demonstrated a new type of molecularly imprinted fluorescent porous nanoparticles with potential applications for selective recognition of PTX biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Paclitaxel (PTX) is a cell replication inhibitor with strong anticancer activity extracted from the scorpion yew, which can be used for the treatment of various cancers such as breast cancer, acute leukemia and ovarian cancer [1–4]. It blocks the late G2 and M phases of the cell cycle on tubulin and has anti-mitotic activity Paclitaxel is concentration-dependent in the induction of cell death [5,6]. Studies on paclitaxel have shown that plasma concentrations in patients treated are up to three times more variability, and that treatment often causes a series of serious side effects such as nausea and vomiting, leukopenia, and so on. Therefore, it is very important to monitor them during treatment [7]. Various methods have been developed to trace the concentration of paclitaxel in human plasma samples, such as high performance liquid chromatography [8,9]. However, this method has a lot of drawbacks in the recognition of paclitaxel, such as high production costs, tedious preparation procedures and poor chemical stability [10]. Therefore, it is important to develop a new low-cost detection sensor for selective recognition of PTX.

Over the past decade, the ﬂuorescence sensors have received increasing attention, because of its simplicity, and high efﬁciency [11]. The use of ﬂuorescence methods in chemical and bioanalysis has become popular largely these days. Among many fluorescent dyes, quantum dots (QDs) possess plenty of advantages including bright photoluminescence, good photo stability, long life time, high quantum yields and low back-ground noise [12–15]. Up to now, a variety of QD-based sensors have been prepared by non-covalent force. However, selectivity remains a challenge for QDs based probes. To improve the selective recognition of QD-based sensors, molecularly imprinted technique has been introduced as a common method to recognition units [16–18]. By combining the merits of high sensitivity of ﬂuorescence sensor and the high selectivity of MIPs, the molecularly imprinted ﬂuorescent sensors can play an important role in molecular recognition field [19,20]. Up until now, some researchers have reported several studies of MIPs in ﬂuorescence sensors, which have been used for the detection of phycocyanin [21], 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin [22], proteins [23], and sparfloxacin [24].

However, there are many disadvantages in current ﬂuorescent imprinted sensors, particularly in terms of slow response times [25]. Generally, the contact between the fluorescent imprinted sensor and the target molecule is not rapid, it takes longer time for the target molecule to enter in the imprinting position, eventually resulting in a longer time for optical detection of the target molecule [26]. As is well known, POSS is an organic-inorganic hybrid nanomaterial, porous POSS-based materials can accelerate the speed of the imprinted molecules into the position recognition point in the porous material [27]. So many porous POSS-based polymers with high surface areas have been reported through diverse reactions [28,29].

Recently we reported a novel MIP microparticle containing POSS as a carrier for PTX delivery [30]. As a continuation of work, we report a simple strategy to construct a novel QDs based porous ﬂuorescent sensor for detection of PTX. The MIP-based ﬂuorescent sensor was constructed by grafting MIP layer on the surface of QDs using ZnS QDs as ﬂuorescent support materials, acrylamide as functional monomers, POSS as co-monomer, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as cross-linking agent and PTX as template. In addition, the addition of M-POSS was expected to increase the pore structure and reduce the transfer resistance and helps the target molecule to reach the recognition site quickly. The sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and reusability of the ﬂuorescent sensor were investigated in detail.

ZnSO4⋅7H2O, Na2S⋅9H2O, MnCl2⋅4H2O, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), 3-mercaptopropyltriethoxysilane (MPTS) were obtained from Kermel Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Acrylamide (AM), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), Methacryl-POSS (M-POSS), 2,2′-Azobis-isobutyronitrile (AIBN) and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) was obtained from Aladdin Co. Ltd (Shanghai, china). Paclitaxel (PTX), Docetaxel (DOC), Etoposide (EPS), 10-deacetylbaccatin III (10-DBC) were obtained from Sam Chemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

FT-IR spectra (4000−400 cm−1) are recorded with KBr pellet on a spectrum 100 infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies were analyzed using a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer. The morphological evaluations were taken on Talos-F200X (FEI, USA) transmission electron microscopes. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) were recorded on a K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The specific surface areas and pore structure distribution are estimated by N2 adsorption isotherms (77 K) on Micromeritics ASAP 2020 plus surface area and porosity analyzer. Before the measurement, the impurities are removed at 110 °C by pretreating the samples (0.1−0.2 g) for 10 h. Fluorescence measurements were performed on a LS-55 ﬂuorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Mn: ZnS quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The Mn: ZnS Quantum Dots were prepared by following reported method [31]. First, 25 mM of ZnSO4⋅7H2O, 2 mM of MnCl2⋅4H2O and 80 mL of distilled water were mixed and kept stirring for 30 min. Next, 10 mL of a 25 mmol Na2S⋅9H2O aqueous solution was added, and after stirring one hour, 0.3 mmol of MPTS and 5 mL of ethanol was added to the mixture, and the mixture was further reacted under stirring for 24 h. The product modified ZnS QDs were washed three times with water and ethanol.

The synthesis of amino surface-modified Mn:ZnS QDs was according to the reported method [31]. Briefly, first 1 mL of QDs and 0.2 ml NH3H2O were added into 22.5 mL of EtOH with vigorously stirring for 1 h. Then 0.8 mL of TEOS was added, continuously stirring for 4 h. Finally, 1 mL of APTES was added to the mixture, and the reaction was further stirred for 24 h. The product was washed three times with ethanol and named QDs@SiO2.

Synthesis of POSS-MIP/ QDs was carried out as follows. Commonly, 0.4 mmol of AM, 1.8 mmol of EGDMA, 100 mg of QDs@SiO2 and 42.7 mg of PTX were mixed in 100 mL of acetonitrile, prepolymerized in the dark for 12 h to fully prepolymerized. Then, 0.04 mmol of POSS and 10 mg of AIBN were added to the mixture by ultrasonic vibration. After purging with N2 for 20 min, the mixture reacted at 60 °C for 20 h. The product was centrifuged (6000 rpm), and then centrifuged three times by ultrasonic methanol washing to dislodge the monomer and template which did not react completely. Afterwards, resulting solid powder were dried overnight at 60 °C. The POSS-NIP/QDs was prepared following the same way, except without the PTX. To further explore the performance of imprinted polymers with POSS, MIP/QDs and NIP/QDs was also prepared in the same way, except that no co-monomer POSS was added.

Then 250 mL methanol/acetic acid (19:1, V:V) mixed solution was prepared, and the template molecules were removed by Soxhlet extraction method until the template molecules could not be detected by UV. Finally, wash to neutral with ethanol and distilled water, and vacuum dry overnight.

The fluorescence measurements were performed at λex =310 nm with a slit width of 10 nm for excitation and recording emission range is at 500−700 nm. POSS-MIP/QDs or POSS-NIP/QDs were added into the PTX solution (acetonitrile /H2O 4/1) at the certain concentration of 0.1 g L−1. The mixture was stirring for 1 h for detection. Short-wavelength cutoff filter was utilized in the fluorescent measurements.

The fabrication process of POSS-MIP/QDs were illustrated Fig. 1. As seen, silane functionalized Mn: ZnS Quantum Dots were prepared and modified via sol-gel method which performed as fluorescent signal source. Then, after removing the template molecule, POSS-MIP/QDs with corresponding imprinting sites were obtained by free radical polymerization using Am as functional monomer, POSS as co-monomer and EGDMA as cross-linking agent. The MIP imprinting shell protects the quantum dots, while avoiding interference from other impurity molecules. A certain proportion of POSS was selected as a mesoporous building unit to obtain the mesoporous structure for further improving the permeability of the surface imprinted layer. The amount of POSS added was based on our previous study [30], and 10 % of the number of functional monomers was selected as the addition amount, while MIP had great imprinting factor and had a large specific surface area. In order to investigate the changes in the response capability of POSS to the materials, MIP/QDs and NIP/QDs were prepared without POSS.

The FT-IR spectra of QDs, QDs@SiO2, MIP/QDs and POSS-MIP/QDs are shown in Fig. 2 . As seen from Fig. 2b, compared with QDs, the characteristic peaks of the stretching vibration of CH are around 2918 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1, which is obviously higher than ZnS QDs. The absorbance peaks of amino groups at 1400 cm−1. These results indicate that APTES is successfully grafted on the surface of QDs@SiO2. Meanwhile, it can be found that MIP/QDs and POSS-MIP/QDs show similar characteristic peaks due to the similar composition. As are shown in Fig. 2c and 2d, the peaks at around 1731 cm−1 and 1732 cm−1 could be assigned as the stretching vibration of CO, and 2986 cm−1 and 2956 cm−1 could be assigned as the vibration of saturated CH, both confirming that EGDMA and AM are successfully polymerized onto the surface of QDs@SiO2. The Si-O-Si vibration in the POSS may overlap with the signal peak of the SiO2 core. All the bonds evidence further supports the successful synthesis of MIP/QDs and POSS-MIP/QDs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to investigate the morphological structures of QDs, QDs@SiO2 and POSS-MIP/QDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the Mn:ZnS QDs are uniformly distributed and the average particle diameter is about 3 nm. The particle size of QDs@SiO2 obtained by encapsulating SiO2 with QDs is still small, with a diameter of about 18−28 nm (Fig. 3b). Compared with QDs@SiO2, it is apparent that the POSS-MIP/QDs particles are significantly increased to about 150−200 nm. The MIP particles exhibit a spherical core-shell structure with many wrinkles on the surface and good dispersion. The imprinted layer is successfully wrapped onto the surface of SiO2, and it can be found from the high-resolution TEM image (HRTEM) that the imprinted shell layer is thin and the average thickness is between 10 nm and 35 nm (Fig. 3c). The ultra-thin imprinted layer can greatly shorten the response time of the sensor as well as improve sensor sensitivity.

In addition, element mapping analysis is performed to demonstrate that the POSS reagent successfully participate in the polymerization process on selected regions of the dark field image in Fig. 3d. As shown in Fig. 3e–i, it is found that both S element and Zn element are distributed in the SiO2 core. Si element is not only distributed in the SiO2 core, but also evenly distributed in the imprinted shell. This indicates that the POSS reagent is successfully dispersedly involved in the polymerization process. The above results indicate that POSS-MIP/QDs nanoparticles have been successfully prepared.

The X-ray diffraction pattern was used to further study the purity and crystal structure of nanoparticles (Fig. 4). The three characteristic peaks of the Mn:ZnS QDs indicate that they are cubic sphalerite structures [32], and these peaks are lower after coating the imprinted layer, which indicate the successful imprinting procedures. XPS spectrum was carried out to investigate the the molecular structure of the material. As are shown in Fig. 5 a, 1021 eV, 1044 eV, and 161 eV represent Zn2p3, Zn2p1, and S2p, respectively. The presence of characteristic peaks of these two elements indicate the successful synthesis of Mn:ZnS QDs. The appearance and enhancement of Si, N1s, C and O peaks in Fig. 5b mark the surface of the QDs successfully modified APTES. Then there are much obvious and consistent C1s and O1s peaks at 285 eV and 532 eV, indicating that the MIP/QDs and POSS-MIP/QDs polymer shells are successfully wrapped on the surface of QDs@SiO2.

The porosity of these materials was investigated by N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms at 77.3 K. As shown in Table 1 the specific surface areas of POSS-MIP/QDs and POSS-NIP/QDs are 247.9 m2 g−1 and 261.7 m2 g−1, respectively, which are significantly larger than that of MIP/QDs and NIP/QDs (66.5 m2 g−1 and 102.1 m2 g−1). It can be observed that the specific surface area of both MIP materials is smaller than the specific surface area of the corresponding NIP, which is possibly caused by the addition of template molecules and occupying some sites [33,34]. The typical nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of these materials are depicted in Fig. 6 . The pore dimension distributions show the pore diameters of the materials we prepared are all around 2∼5 nm. In addition, the micropore structures of POSS-MIP/QDs and POSS-NIP/QDs are enormously increased compared with the material without POSS. These results indicate that the presence of POSS reagents increase the pore structure of MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization on the fluorescence properties of the POSS-MIP/QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The environment is an important factor in the adsorption of imprinted polymers. Therefore, the fluorescence detection conditions such as pH and response time of POSS-MIP were optimized [35]. Research has shown that pH affects the dissociation of PTX, which affects the surface charge distribution of POSS-MIP/QDs, and then the fluorescence emission intensity and the recombination of PTX. As shown in Fig. 7 , the changes of the fluorescence emission of the material in the range of pH 4.0∼9.0 were investigated. Under acidic conditions, the fluorescence intensity is weak, and with the increase of pH, the fluorescence intensity of MIP/QDs and NIP/QDs is gradually enhanced. To evaluate fluorescence quenching, (F0-F)/F is defined as an indicator of fluorescence quenching ability. Under acidic conditions, POSS-MIP/QDs have poor quenching ability, while at pH = 7, (F0-F)/F is the largest, and with the increase of alkalinity, the quenching efficiency decreases slightly. Therefore, the subsequent experiment was carried out under the conditions of pH = 7.

In order to evaluate the effect of the porous structure formed by the POSS reagent of the POSS-MIP/QDs on the response time of the imprinted material, the response times of the POSS-MIP/QDs and MIP/QDs sensors for PTX were also tested. As shown in Fig. 8 , within 0−20 min, the fluorescence intensity of the POSS-MIP/QDs and MIP/QDs both decrease significantly, after which the curve become ﬂat. This phenomenon indicates that the sensor has a thinner imprinting layer and the imprinting layer has good contactability and mass transfer efficiency. In addition, POSS-MIP/QDs show greater quenching efficiency than MIP/QDs at the same concentration. The reason is that the presence of a lot of micropores and mesopores facilitates the rapid penetration of the template molecules, enabling rapid detection [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the POSS-MIP/QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The ability of MIP to quantitatively measure PTX was further examined under optimized conditions. The fluorescence quenching follows the Stern-Volmer equation [37].
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
S V
C q
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the QDs in the absence and presence of the template, respectively. K sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and C q is the quencher concentration. The ratio of K sv,MIP to K sv,NIP is defined as the imprinting factor (IF).

As seen in Fig. 9, these materials exhibited quenching responses toward PTX. With increasing PTX concentration, POSS-MIP/QDs and MIP/QDs show a decrease in fluorescence intensity, and POSS-MIP/QDs show good linearity relationship in 0.1−2 μM., correlation coefficient is 0.9926 (Fig. 9a). Based on 3σ/s, the detection limit (LOD) of POSS-MIP/QDs is 0.043 μM; and correspondingly, MIPs maintain a good linear relationship in 0.1−1 μM (correlation coefficient 0.9922). As concentration increases, a nonlinear change occurs, and the performance is worse than the former (Fig. 9c). The possible reason is speculated that the porous structure formed by the addition of POSS allows more target molecules to penetrate to the imprinting sites, which in turn leads to greater fluorescence quenching. As seen in Fig. 9b and d, the fluorescence intensity of POSS-NIP/QDs and NIP/QDs decreased slightly at the same concentration of PTX, and the imprinting factors of POSS-MIP/QDs and MIP/QDs are calculated to be 3.19 and 1.9, respectively. These results indicate that POSS modified MIP/QDs fluorescent imprinted sensors can effectively improve selectivity and sensitivity.

To investigate the selectivity of the POSS-MIP/QDs, the competitive molecules including DOC, EPS and 10-DBC with similar molecular structures to PTX were tested (Fig. 10 ). We measure the ﬂuorescent responses of POSS-MIP/QDs and POSS-NIP/QDs toward PTX and its analogues. As shown from Fig. 10, the ﬂuorescence quenching amount of the POSS-MIP/QDs is the highest for PTX. The quenching of PTX and DOC is almost the same, which is mainly due to the highly similar molecular structure and shape of PTX and DOC, and consistent hydrogen bonding sites. The quenching amplitudes of EPS and 10-DBC are both low, indicating that imprinted polymers take no selectivity for them, probably due to their distinct spatial structure and lack of suitable complementary recognition sites, which makes it difficult to reach the imprinted hole to form corresponding complexes. Correspondingly, POSS-NIP/QDs show low fluorescence quenching efficiency for PTX and three similar compounds. Therefore, POSS-MIP/QDs can be used as a highly selective sensor for PTX and even DOC.

The stability of POSS-MIP/QDs was studied by the repeated detections of the ﬂuorescence signal. After 9-times measurements within 90 min, the results show slight impact on the ﬂuorescence signal of POSS-MIP/QDs. In addition, in order to estimate the stability and reusable performance of the material, the recycling experiments of POSS-MIP/QDs for PTX sorption are investigated. As shown in Fig. 11 , after eight regeneration experiments, the amount of adsorption of the POSS-MIP/QDs has hardly decreased, indicating that it is a promising candidate for PTX ﬂuorescence detection.

To conclude, a novel porous ﬂuorescent imprinted sensor for fluorescence determination of PTX was prepared. By adding strong ﬂuorescence QDs into high selectivity MIPs, the resultant nanoparticles demonstrate good sensitivity and selectivity for PTX. At the same time, the result also shows that the addition of M-POSS agent reduces the transfer resistance and helps the target molecule to reach the recognition site quickly. We believe this composite material is suitable for the analysis of PTX from complex samples such as natural medical plants and biological samples.

Jianwei Bai: Writing - original draft, Formal analysis. Longqi Chen: Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Yawei Zhu: Investigation, Resources. Xiqing Wang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiaodan Wu: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yujie Fu: Resources, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFD060070601-2), S pecial Fund of National Natural Science Foundation of China (31770619), the C hina Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017M621231), Postdoctoral Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LBH-Z16010), the 111 Project ( B20088), and H eilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team).
